title: The eve of the Civil War
description: Late in the 1850s, sectional tension reached a boiling point in the United States. Test your understanding of the major factors: the publication of Uncle Tom's Cabin, the Kansas-Nebraska Act, the Dred Scott decision, and more.
topic_path: The eve of the Civil War

hints: The Underground Railroad network was not a physical railroad.

choices.content: a literal railroad that transported slaves in the South to freedom in the North.
an informal network of abolitionists and other advocates helping slaves escape to freedom.
a spy network informally instituted along with Fugitive Slave Law to help bring escaped slaves back to their owners.

assessment_items.question_content:
By repealing the Missouri Compromise of 1820, which drew the horizontal line of slavery across the West along the 36 30’ parallel, the **Kansas-Nebraska Act**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Even with the repeal of the Missouri Compromise, northern states were not in danger of becoming slave states.

choices.content: reopened the possibility of slavery to expand to new territories north of the Missouri Compromise line.
opened up all states to a vote on the legality of slavery, causing Northern abolitionists to fear that Massachusetts, Ohio, and Pennsylvania could become slave states.
made slavery illegal in Kansas and Nebraska, infuriating white Southerners who believed that they would have been slave states under the terms of the Missouri Compromise.

assessment_items.question_content:
Who created the proslavery **Lecompton Constitution?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Lecompton Constitution was not connected with the Confederate States of America.

choices.content: “Border ruffians” from Missouri who crossed the border to vote for the legalization of slavery in Kansas
The Confederate States of America, who adopted the Lecompton Constitution in lieu of the Constitution of the United States
Mexico; after its adoption Texas broke away and joined the United States so it could be a free state

assessment_items.question_content:
Upon meeting her, why did Abraham Lincoln (allegedly) say to **Harriet Beecher Stowe**,

> “So you’re the little lady who started this great war!”



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Stowe did not advocate for the institution of slavery.

choices.content: Stowe’s novel Uncle Tom’s Cabin had brought abolitionist sentiment into the mainstream, increasing sectional tension to the breaking point.
Stowe’s advocacy of the institution of slavery through widely-published tracts and pamphlets had prompted Southern states to secede from the Union.
The Supreme Court case about Stowe (an enslaved woman who had crossed to a northern state with her owner and then unsuccessfully sued him for freedom) had outraged abolitionists in the North.

assessment_items.question_content:
What did **John Brown **believe about slavery and abolition?



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Slavery could only be abolished through violent means: armed uprising.
Slavery should be abolished through lobbying to change the laws.
Slavery should never be abolished.

assessment_items.question_content:
What did John Brown attempt to do at the federal armory in **Harper’s Ferry**, Virginia?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Brown was not looking to delay war.

choices.content: Steal weapons in order to arm enslaved people for a mass insurrection
Destroy armaments in order to prevent or delay the outbreak of the Civil War
Blockade himself within the armory, taking weapons hostage in exchange for his sons’ release from prison

assessment_items.question_content:
The conflict over whether Kansas would be admitted to the Union as a free or slave state earned the name **“Bleeding Kansas.”** What was the major event that started the violence?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Nat Turner’s rebellion took place in Virginia, not Kansas.

choices.content: Anti-slavery activist John Brown organized the murder of five proslavery settlers.
Proslavery militias crossed into Northern states and murdered abolitionists.
A major slave revolt led by Nat Turner killed more than 60 whites.

assessment_items.question_content:
What was “**popular sovereignty**” in the context of the Kansas-Nebraska Act?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The federal government did not leave it up to territories to decide whether or not they wanted to enter the Union; territories converted to new states were required to enter the Union.

choices.content: Popular sovereignty left it up to the citizens living in each territory to decide whether that territory would enter the Union as a slave state or a free state.
Popular sovereignty left it up to the citizens of each territory to negotiate their own treaties with Native Americans.
Popular sovereignty left it up to the citizens of new territories to decide whether they wanted to enter the Union or organize as independent nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Why did **Preston Brooks** attack **Charles Sumner** with a cane on the Senate floor?

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Sumner had insulted Brooks’s elderly uncle in a vitriolic speech against proslavery advocates.
Brooks mistakenly believed that Sumner had spread a malicious rumor about his wife.
Sumner had suggested that abolitionists such as Brooks were thieves and hypocrites.
